sections.about: This metric measures groundwater abstraction and is part of BRSR Principle 6 Essential Indicator water withdrawal by source. In steelmaking, groundwater dependence is sensitive because aquifer stress is location-specific and regulatory risk can be high. A lower value indicates better performance.
sections.performanceAndDrivers: SAIL reports zero groundwater withdrawal across all three years, and Jindal reaches zero in FY2025 after 19,102 kL in FY2024. Tata remains the clear laggard in groundwater dependence, although it improves from 13.30 million kL in FY2024 to 12.38 million kL in FY2025. JSW’s groundwater withdrawal remains small in absolute terms versus Tata but worsens from 15,142 kL in FY2023 to 118,140 kL in FY2025. Jindal’s FY2025 BRSR table confirms zero groundwater withdrawal and total withdrawal of 15.08 million kL, with reasonable assurance by SGS.
sections.tataPositioning: In FY2025, Tata ranks 4th. SAIL and Jindal are best at zero, so the percentage gap is not meaningful; Tata’s absolute gap from the leaders is 12.38 million kL. Tata’s rank remains worst across the period, even though FY2024-25 shows operational improvement.
sections.targets: Tata’s 4R water framework includes reducing freshwater withdrawal from rivers and maximising recycling, but no groundwater-specific quantitative target was identified. SAIL’s ZLD goal indirectly reduces freshwater and groundwater dependence.
sections.comparabilityNotes: Tata Steel FY2022-23 groundwater withdrawal is reported for Tata Steel India and its Indian subsidiaries, not on a purely standalone basis. Groundwater risk should ideally be assessed with water-stress basin overlays, not only absolute kL.
